era_context: 746 CE is in the final years of the Umayyad Caliphate, the height of Tang Dynasty power, the Carolingian consolidation in the Frankish kingdoms, and the Byzantine iconoclastic period. This year may mark the beginning of the Abbasid Revolution's public phase, though sources differ on the exact dating.

events.title: Abbasid Revolution begins public phase (alternative dating); Abu Muslim Khorasani raises black banners
events.description: In 746 CE (or possibly 747), Abu Muslim Khorasani may have publicly launched the Abbasid Revolution by raising the black banners in Khorasan. This marked the transition from underground conspiracy to open rebellion against Umayyad rule. Abu Muslim's forces began openly recruiting followers and engaging in military campaigns against Umayyad authorities. The black banners became the symbol of the Abbasid movement, and the revolution spread rapidly through the eastern Islamic provinces. The exact date of this event is uncertain, with sources varying between 746 and 747.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Abbasid Revolution's public phase is traditionally dated to 747 CE, but some sources place it in 746. The exact date is uncertain, and sources differ by a year or two. The event itself is confirmed in later Islamic historiography.
cross_references: Leads to 750-001 (Battle of the Great Zab)

events.title: Tang Dynasty at height of power; military operations in Central Asia
events.description: In 746 CE, the Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong was at the height of its power and cultural influence. The Tang court at Chang'an was a centre of cosmopolitan culture, and the Tang military was dominant in Central Asia. The Silk Road trade was flourishing, and Tang influence extended across a vast territory. However, the overextension of Tang military resources and the reliance on non-Han generals would soon become problematic.
events.certainty_note: The Tang Dynasty's power and Xuanzong's reign are well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 746 is a conventional marker within this period.

events.title: Pepin III consolidates Frankish power; preparations for formal kingship
events.description: In 746 CE, Pepin III (the Short) was consolidating his power as mayor of the palace under the nominal Merovingian king Childeric III. Pepin was conducting military campaigns and administrative reforms to strengthen the Frankish state. The Merovingian dynasty was in its final decline, with real power held by the Carolingian mayors of the palace. Pepin's actions were laying the groundwork for his formal assumption of kingship in 751.
events.certainty_note: Pepin III's rise to power is well-attested, but the precise chronology of events in 746 is unclear. Sources are not contemporary.

events.title: Byzantine iconoclasm under Constantine V; religious persecution continues
events.description: In 746 CE, Emperor Constantine V continued his enforcement of iconoclasm across the Byzantine Empire. Icon-venerating monks were persecuted, religious artworks were destroyed, and the theological debate over the legitimacy of religious images remained intense. Constantine V's policies were supported by the imperial bureaucracy and military but opposed by the populace, the monastic establishment, and the papacy.
events.certainty_note: Constantine V's iconoclastic policies are well-attested. The year 746 falls within the documented period of his enforcement.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the beginning of the Abbasid Revolution's public phase is uncertain; sources vary between 746 and 747. The notion that the revolution was inevitable by 746 is retrospective; at the time, the outcome was uncertain. The characterisation of the Abbasid Revolution as a clear 'progress' toward a more inclusive Islamic state is disputed by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: 746 CE is poorly documented compared to 750 CE. Most information about this year is reconstructed from broader narratives of longer-term processes. Islamic sources are dominated by al-Tabari and later compilers, who may have compressed or distorted events. The dating of the Abbasid Revolution's public phase varies by source. Chinese sources are abundant but court-sponsored. Western European sources are sparse. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Indian subcontinent, and Oceania remain largely absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: The public launch of the Abbasid Revolution in 746 (or 747) led to the decisive Battle of the Great Zab in 750
